Sullivan Robey kicked long into the Bombers' attacking 50, with only teammate Nate Caddy and the Lions' Ty Gallop near the ball. Caddy won the race to the pill before dribbling it through the big sticks for his second goal. Neale cancels out Bombers' major L By Luke Pentony It didn't take the Lions long to shift back into top gear. Lachie Neale joined the goal-kickers' list for the Lions, restoring their 35-point lead. Blakiston gives Bombers hope L By Luke Pentony Credit to the Bombers, they didn't hang their heads after Logan Morris gave the Lions their latest goal. The Bombers responded on the scoreboard almost immediately, with Lachlan Blakiston slotting his side's third major. Lions make Bombers pay L By Luke Pentony Logan Morris has a third goal for the afternoon. The Bombers were on the attack when Andrew McGrath had a handpass intercepted by Kai Lohmann just outside the home side's defensive 50. Lohmann got the ball to Morris, who kicked towards goal and came away with another major. The Lions by 35. Morris has a second for Lions L By Luke Pentony Logan Morris gave another glimpse of his enormous potential with his second goal of the match. Morris was on the lead inside the attacking 50 to create a target for Sam Draper. Draper connected to give Morris a set-shot chance, which he converted. Bombers might be listening L By Luke Pentony It should be a little bit better than that Essendon Bombers in the 1st Quarter my little advice on this one to covert your kicking into goals and plus try to get into them a little bit more and tackle them to the ground and spread around the ground a bit more and pressure them in that way you’ll get turnovers and Free kicks out form it too. - Christiane Bombers make positive start L By Luke Pentony That's more like it from the Bombers! Nate Caddy took an outstanding mark close to goal, before nailing the set-shot after just 52 seconds of play in the second term. Still a long way to go for the Bombers, but it's the start they needed after the quarter-time break. Second quarter gets going L By Luke Pentony Play resumes under the roof at Docklands, with the Lions enjoying a 29-point lead against the Bombers. QT: Lions 6.2 (38) lead Bombers 1.3 (9) L By Luke Pentony (Getty) It was all the Lions in the first quarter against the Bombers, kicking six goals to one. Can the Bombers respond in the second term? Lions move further ahead L By Luke Pentony Logan Morris took an excellent contested mark close to the sticks, before converting from the set-shot that followed. The Lions have SIX goals already this term. Can the Bombers find a response? Rayner gets in on the fun L By Luke Pentony The Lions now have five goals, with Cam Rayner the latest to kick truly. Their lead has stretched to 24 points at the midway point of the first term. Lions have a fourth goal to their name L By Luke Pentony The Lions capitalise on some lazy Bombers defence to claim a fourth goal of the opening quarter. Darcy Fort appeared to be preparing to take a set-shot just outside of the arc, but instead noticed the Bombers' defence weren't paying attention to Conor McKenna inside 50. Fort kicked to McKenna, who marked and slotted a major for the Lions. Bailey doubles up L By Luke Pentony Zac Bailey has his second goal of the quarter for the Lions. Charlie Cameron played distributor, chipping a kick inside 50 to find Bailey. Bailey made no mistake from the set-shot, establishing a two-goal lead for the visitors. Bombers open account L By Luke Pentony A free kick to the Bombers, inside their attacking 50, gave Peter Wright the chance to kick his side's first goal. Wright comfortably completed the assignment, reducing the Lions' lead to a kick. Support for Bombers L By Luke Pentony Good luck Essendon Bombers right behind you on this one. My little advice what’s the one thing Brisbane Lions are good in the Midfield Area and Full -Forward Line and down back you need to match them in all areas of the ground as a Sydney Swans supporter. - Christiane Cameron adds to Lions' lead L By Luke Pentony Charlie Cameron goes around the corner from a set-shot in the pocket to kick the Lions' second goal of the match. Early signs aren't promising for the Bombers. Lions on the board early L By Luke Pentony The Lions already have a goal to their name after just one minute in the first quarter. The Bombers gave away a free kick inside their defensive 50, allowing Zac Bailey an easy set-shot opportunity, which he didn't waste. Away we go! L By Luke Pentony Play is underway, with the lid on at Docklands. Will the Lions bank a win on the road? Or can the Bombers lift after last weekend's heavy defeat to the Pies? Let's find out! Well played, Lions! L By Luke Pentony Show more posts

title: Why Dee Salmin thinks her book could end relationships
text:
Dee Salmin laughs when asked whether she believes her new book will lead to relationship bust-ups around Australia.

"I low-key would love that!" the podcast host tells triple j hack.

"But it's not because I want to break people up.

"However, if reading it … makes some women reflect on why their relationship isn't as fulfilling as it should be … then I've done my job."

Salmin's book, It's Not Love, Actually, is jam-packed with advice, reflections and anecdotes from a life of dating, sex and relationships that range from the ridiculous to the traumatic.

Loading...

But the broadcaster is quick to point out that the book differs significantly from the dating guides she devoured from her teenage years, right through her twenties.

"They always positioned men and finding a relationship as the goal," she says.

"The period of being single was always a small little chapter and it was like, 'just wait, it'll happen to you one day and try and keep yourself busy until then.'

"This is the opposite."

Being the 'cool girl'

When it comes to relationship advice, it's difficult to imagine a more self-assured advocate for women than Salmin.

The host of triple j's sex and relationship show, 'The Hook Up,' she is also known for her bold outfit choices, advocacy for sustainability, and for calling out — and sometimes owning –the labels from online trolls.

"My favourite is 'gonad-stealing feminist witch'," she jokes.

"It's in the Instagram bio."

Loading Instagram content

But Salmin admits the confidence wasn't always there.

"I had been single for most of my life and I really struggled with that.

"I'd seen so many people around me have boyfriends, have sex and date and I felt like there was something wrong with me."

Salmin says the pressure to hook up and get a boyfriend drove her to extreme lengths.

In an effort to appear as compatible as possible, she became the "cool girl" who loved everything.

"I would mould myself to whatever the guys were," she tells hack.

"If they were a surfer, I was the cool, chill beachy babe.

"If it was the guy who studied politics, I would go out and read all the books on politics.

"I would always be up for everything and never had any needs of my own."

'I felt so much shame'

It's taken years for Salmin to realise just how much trauma she has endured.

At the age of 14 she was sexually assaulted.

But it wasn't until she was in her twenties, speaking with a friend, that Salmin recognised the experience for what it was.

"What he had done to her, he had done to me … we were both in that bed," she tells triple j hack.

" It was horrific to hear your friend so rattled and hurt by that experience, but then it was also so validating at the same time. "

Salmin recalls that she tried to minimise the assault when it happened.

"I felt so much shame and then tried to own it and be like, 'I wanted it to happen'."

"That's because women are taught that sex is something that happens to them … that women are just the receivers of men's pleasure."

The crash that changed everything

When asked about a pivotal moment that made her re-think her views on relationships, Salmin opens up about one of the hardest periods of her life.

At 18, she found the relationship she had been looking for.

Dee Salmin says her friends showed her what love is after a serious car crash when she was 18. (Supplied)

"It was my first love and I thought this would be my person forever."

But Salmin's life was up-ended when the two were involved in a car accident in regional Western Australia.

Salmin was driving when the car hit a tree, trapping her inside.

Suffering serious injuries to her legs and face, she was eventually cut free from the car and flown to Perth for emergency treatment.

"I was told I might not ever walk again.

"I was in a wheelchair for six months and then on a walking frame for like a year."

But Salmin says it was the emotional consequences of the crash that were hardest to deal with.

She expected her partner to be there but instead, he disappeared.

"I didn't see him for six weeks," she tells hack.

"He never called or messaged me.

" I was more hurt by that experience with him than I was about the idea that I might never walk again. "

But Salmin says the unwavering support from her friends in the immediate aftermath of the accident taught her more about love than anything she's learned since.

"I realised we have been prioritising romantic love the wrong way.

"It's dangerous to make your romantic partner your absolutely everything; your best friend, your soulmate.

"It's really set a lot of us up for failure."

'Embrace yourself'

Salmin is encouraging women to prioritise themselves, reclaim dating and sex, and embrace single life, rather than fixating on finding a partner.

Dee Salmin says it is dangerous to make your partner your whole world. (Supplied)

Whether it's going 'boy sober' — as she did for three years during the COVID pandemic — de-centring men, or thinking about dating as an opportunity to get to know people rather than finding a partner, Salmin is urging women to embrace their passions and get to know themselves.

"I had a blank space and I just wanted to fill it," she says.

"That's the wrong approach.

"I think it's important to learn about yourself and other people."

Dating Darcy Moore

When it comes to the most scrutinised part of her personal life, Salmin says she intentionally did not dedicate much space in her book to the topic.

"I do not want people to assume that this is a book of 'how to get your Darcy Moore'," she says.

"It is not."

Magpies captain Darcy Moore and partner Triple J presenter Dee Salmin. (Getty Images: Morgan Hancock )

Salmin has been in a high-profile relationship with the Collingwood captain for three years.

"Having a bit of that spotlight has just brought us closer together because it's made us have these really big important discussions about our relationship and how we want to protect it," she says.

"I think you're forced to have bigger conversations quicker."

Salmin says while she loves Moore immensely, they share a sense of independence, which means both of them understand that dedicating time to themselves is crucial.

"The longest relationship you will ever have in your life is the relationship with yourself, so why wouldn't you invest in that?

"If my romantic relationship was to end, of course I would be devastated and heartbroken.

"But I know that I've got myself and I know that I would be happy again and I would have just as fulfilling a life without him.

"It's been a journey."

title: Retro 1980s train returns to tracks after major refurbishment
text:
On a quiet weekday in the town of Kendall, a recorded railway announcement cuts through the still morning air, heralding the XPT's imminent arrival.

At the New South Wales town's heritage-listed railway station, eager rail travellers – and a few trainspotters – cheer and wave as the iconic train rolls to a stop.

It is the first run of the XPT (eXpress Passenger Train) service between Sydney and the north coast town of Grafton, after a year off the tracks for major upgrades.

To mark the relaunch, the first service this week carried the iconic heritage colours that defined the XPT when it first began in the early 1980s.

"We know so many in the community love the XPT and we have a huge rail enthusiast community across the state who love the XPT," NSW TrainLink chief executive Roger Weeks said.

The XPT arriving at Kendall Railway Station with its retro colours. (ABC News: Emma Siossian)

" So, we put it out in its original, and many would say best livery, and that's what is lovingly known as the candy stripe. "

Brian Hudson, 85, a former NSW station master with a 36-year rail career, was among those to greet the train.

Brian Hudson, a former station master, waits to greet the refurbished XPT at Kendall. (ABC News: Emma Siossian)

Mr Hudson started at the Kendall station in 1957, operating the rail crossing gates before eventually progressing to assistant station master at Kendall, and later, a second-class station master in Sydney.

"It's marvellous to see the XPT back ... it's the sort of rail travel I enjoy ... the comfort, the relaxation, you don't have to worry about anything," he said.

"When I first started there were only two electric diesel engines operating in Australia, all the others were steam … the steam trains used to fill up all the time with water."

Rail traveller Irene Choo was among those boarding the Sydney-bound XPT at Kendall, delighted to have the service back.

"It's so exciting, it's great," she said.

"It's so much more convenient and it's safer for me … compared to coaches, you can actually get up and walk around and it's comfortable and they have food on the train."

The refurbished XPT on its journey through the countryside at Kendall, NSW. (Supplied: Matthew Doyle)

Port Macquarie photographer Matthew Doyle was among those out trainspotting as the XPT passed through Kendall.

"The new updated livery brings back many memories for people across the state, who grew up travelling on and seeing the XPT trains pass through their towns," he said.

"I remember travelling on it to school camp when I was a teenager, looking exactly like this. It's pretty special."

XPT still on track

The XPT first transported paying customers from Sydney, over the Blue Mountains and to NSW's central west in 1982.

The North Coast route between Sydney and Brisbane is the longest XPT service, covering 1,012 kilometres over 15 hours.

An XPT in service in June 1986. (Supplied: Transport for NSW)

A Transport for NSW project is underway to replace the aging XPT fleet with new Regional Rail bi-mode trains, for people travelling across NSW and between Sydney, Canberra, Melbourne and Brisbane.

They will be the first passenger trains in Australia capable of using both diesel power and overhead power when operating on the electrified train network, to reduce emissions.

A 1980s XPT pamphlet. (Supplied: NSW TrainLink)

They will be able to reach speeds of up to 160 kilometres per hour, similar to the XPT, subject to factors including track infrastructure and network speeds.

There is no firm start date for their introduction with testing still underway, so the XPT fleet has undergone a $40.3 million life-extension refurbishment.

"That means this iconic train will continue to be fit for service for many years to come," Mr Weeks said.

"Because [the new train] is the first of its type in Australia, the testing we need to do to satisfy the regulator is very extensive."

Lure of the railway

The refurbished XPT makes its way north through Wirragulla in the NSW Hunter region. (Supplied: Lewis Jagger)

Mr Weeks said train travel remained popular in Australia, especially as people sought cheaper alternatives to road travel amid soaring fuel prices.

In March there were about 113,500 total journeys on NSW TrainLink's long-distance train network across regional NSW and interstate, which is about 3.6 per cent more than March 2025.

"We have seen our numbers grow to beyond what we were carrying before COVID, so after the pandemic we have seen a very strong return to our regional and interstate rail services," Mr Weeks said.

" In the current fuel crisis we are seeing across all our public transport modes, a slight increase, as people are making the decision to use public transport. "

Train driver Stephen Daymond at Sydney's Central Station, ahead of the first Sydney to Grafton run of the refurbished XPT. (Supplied: Transport for NSW)

After the initial run on the Grafton route, the retro-painted XPT train will be used across the broader NSW network, as well as services to Brisbane and Melbourne.

"We are going to send it around the network so that rail enthusiasts, passengers and our regional communities can get to see this amazing train," Mr Weeks said.

title: How a load of 're-pimped' bikes are helping outback kids get to school
text:
'Creative community consultant' Timika King aims to make Meekatharra, in WA's Midwest, a better place to live.

So, spotting a number of damaged, abandoned bikes in her remote hometown, she pursued an idea that has provided kids with new rides and also boosted school attendance.

The town has a population of 850, of which just under half are Indigenous. Ms King herself is a traditional owner.

Community leaders and kids came together for the workshop. (Supplied:Shire of Meekatharra)

She worked with the shire and local youth workers to seek donations of bikes and get them delivered into the right hands.

About 130 people joined a workshop to rebuild and repair 100 donated bikes, which were then passed on to those in need.

"We had community volunteers, uncles, pops, youth workers, aunts and mums," she said.

"Everyone just chucked in, put chains on, put new handlebars on.

" Some of them [bikes] needed a bit of re-pimping, so we did the spray paint and stickers, a few tassels, made it all glittery. "

Ms King (left) says some of the donated bikes needed "re-pimping". (Supplied: Shire of Meekatharra)

More kids at school

Ms King said the project had had a "massive" impact on the young people who were able to keep the bikes.

With around 100 students, Meekatharra's only school had an attendance rate of less than 40 per cent last year, compared to WA's average of 85 per cent.

Around 100 students attend the outback school. (ABC Midwest & Wheatbelt: Chloe Henville)

The Department of Education could not provide current student attendance data, but Ms King said there had been a visible increase in students making their own way to school.

"The school has an allocated area for push bikes to be stored, and that went from empty to full overnight," she said.

"We saw heaps more kids riding to school."

Local Youth Focus service manager Paulette Anderson agreed, saying more young people were heading to school and getting fresh air thanks to the bikes.

"It's a hell of a lot easier to ride a bike in 45 degree heat than it is to walk," she laughed.

Ms Anderson says the bikes provide equal opportunities for local youth. (ABC News: Jake Sturmer)

Ms Anderson said bike repair work helped teach young people personal accountability.

"It creates a responsibility for them," she said.

"[The kids think] 'this is mine, I've helped fix it, I've helped repair it, so I'm going to look after it.'"

Young people got involved in fixing the donated bikes. (Supplied:Shire of Meekatharra)

Ms Anderson said the workshop also helped foster connections in the community.

"It wasn't just one person doing one thing for a small group of people," she said.

"It's about the very basics of relationship building that takes no effort from the adults at all. We're there, we show up, and the kids can build a positive relationship based on those interactions."

'A good thing to do'

Some of the donated bikes came from Bicycles for Humanity, an international group focused on creating basic mobility for those in need.

It is hoped the bike project will foster community connection in Meekatharra. (ABC Midwest & Wheatbelt: Chloe Henville)

Peter Moore runs the Geraldton branch and said it was fantastic to see the impact of the donation on the kids.

"They need something to do and they take pride in getting a bike and fixing it up and giving it a coat of paint," he said.

"It's a good thing to do, I think."

Mr Moore says local kids have taken pride in the project. (Supplied:Shire of Meekatharra)

title: WA has enough fuel for May amid 'growing confidence' for June, minister says
text:
WA's energy minister says the state will have the fuel it needs for this month and has "growing confidence" for June, as the government examines how it can manage fuel supplies beyond the current crisis.

As the conflict in the Middle East drags on, the availability and the price of fuel remain a concern across Australia.

The federal government said it had secured around 450 million litres of extra fuel since the start of the crisis, including 100 million litres of jet fuel to be shared between Perth and Brisbane, announced yesterday.

Fuel supplies remain a concern as the Middle East conflict drags on. (ABC News: Matthew Roberts)

The WA government has also agreed to purchase 12 million litres of diesel to hold in stockpiles in Wyndham, Kwinana and Esperance, which can be sold into the market to smooth out short-term supply gaps in particular areas.

"We're confident of fuel supply for May and we have growing confidence for June," Energy Minister Amber-Jade Sanderson told Stateline.

"The actions of the federal government, particularly the export finance arrangements, has been one of the most important interventions, I think, in this global crisis because it's signalled to the world that we're willing to pay for fuel, as a nation."

Under those arrangements additional fuel purchases by importers are underwritten by the government, allowing companies to make what would otherwise be risky or unprofitable purchases.

WA considering separate stockpile of diesel amid fuel shortages The WA government is considering creating its own "strategic stockpile" of diesel, saying the state's size and the reliance of the agriculture and resources industries on the fuel warrants a separate reserve.

'Growing confidence'

Ms Sanderson expressed cautious optimism about the outlook beyond June, saying the feedback from fuel suppliers was that "alternative supply chains" were starting to emerge.

"We are starting to see that crude oil that has been produced from other countries outside of the Middle East make its way into refineries destined for Australia and Western Australia," she said.

Amber-Jade Sanderson said the government was preparing for several outcomes amid the uncertainty. (ABC News: Benjamin Gubana )

The minister acknowledged the situation was "rapidly changing" and said the state was preparing for multiple scenarios.

This could include moving to a higher level of the National Fuel Security Plan, which could mean further restrictions on fuel supply, if that were agreed to by national cabinet.

"I don't think there's any inevitabilities in this scenario, but what our job is [is] to make sure that we're ready should that be the case," she said.

Those preparations are being made by 26 people brought together into a team from across the public service, some of whom were also involved in managing the state's COVID response, she added.

" That's why we're doing it, because we're making sure that we're in it and prepared for the long haul should this be an extended issue. "

Fuel security act

The state government is also turning its attention to the longer-term challenge of fuel security.

Ms Sanderson used emergency powers to force the state's six fuel suppliers to regularly hand over a range of information about their supply chains including fuel volumes, distribution points and frequency of distribution.

WA Energy Minister Amber-Jade Sanderson announcing the emergency powers that required suppliers to give the government data. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

The minister said that data has been "invaluable" in guiding the government's response, and that it would be a "reasonable suggestion" for authorities to continue receiving that information after current emergency powers expire.

"We have it for the electricity system, we have it for gas supply. It makes sense to me that you would have it, for diesel in particular, which is critical to our industry," she said.

"So we'll certainly be looking into that."

All WA regional stations must report fuel prices from May as FuelWatch scheme expands The FuelWatch reporting scheme is being expanded to all regional fuel stations in Western Australia, with about 200 additional retailers required to report their fuel prices.

Suppliers keen to share data

Some fuel suppliers contacted by the ABC suggested they were open to the idea.

A Viva Energy spokesperson said the company had provided information to the WA government before the activation of emergency powers and would work "productively" to decide what information would be required long-term.

"We would like to see a uniform approach, to ensure consistency, fairness and efficiency across levels of government," they said.

"The government is keen to keep receiving this information in order to remain informed about the status of the fuel supply in WA and we have agreed to continue providing it."

Some petrol stations ran out of supplies during the early days of the Middle East conflict. (ABC News)

Chevron Australia Downstream had a similar position.

"We have agreed for the data we report to the federal government under the Fuel Security Act to be shared with the WA government to assist the state and to reduce the significant reporting overlap," a spokesperson said.

A BP spokesperson said the company had been regularly sharing information and "will continue to comply with any data requests".

Moving away from diesel

Reflecting on how the current crisis would affect government and industry, Ms Sanderson — who is also the state's decarbonisation minister — said it was a reminder of how reliant the state was on diesel.

WA Minister Amber-Jade Sanderson says moving away from fossil fuels is still the goal. (ABC News: Benjamin Gubana )

"It's a reminder that we have to continue to decarbonise and move away from our dependence on diesel and fossil fuels," she said.

"There's time … and we are going to need diesel for some time and into the future, so it's really about making sure we have it where we need it and when we need it, and ensuring that resilience in the future."

title: Super shoes won't be banned despite 'technological doping' claims
text:
World Athletics boss Lord Sebastian Coe said he will not "strangle" the innovation that has led to a new market in super shoes.

However, the two-time Olympic 1,500-metres champion told the BBC that the governing body had a "regulatory responsibility" to the sport.

Super shoes have come under the spotlight again after last weekend's record-breaking London Marathon, where Sabastian Sawe became the first man to run sub-two hours for the 42.2-kilometre distance in an official race.

Not only that, but second-placed Yomif Kejelcha also broke the once-mythical mark, while Tigst Assefa set a new women's only world record in the women's race.

Sabastian Sawe holds up the new Adidas super shoe. Someone once wrote that "it's not about the bike". Athletics' challenge is to ensure it's not about the shoe. (Getty Images: Karwai Tang)

All three were wearing Adidas's new Adizero Adios Pro Evo 3 shoe, the first to weigh less than 100 grams.

Adidas claims the technology within the latest version of its super shoe, including advanced foam and carbon-infused "energy rods" assists in generating high energy return and improves running economy by 1.6 per cent.

There is no doubt we are in a different world to when Ethiopian runner Abebe Bikila set the world record of 2:15.16 on the streets of Rome to win Olympic gold — all the while running barefoot.

But World Athletics is treading a fine line between technological advancement and remaining true to its roots. This is one that swimming, too, once trod, leading to the much-heralded and maligned super-suit era between 2008 and 2010, which saw 200 world records tumble in the space of two years before the cat was wrestled back into the bag and those polyurethane full-body suits were banned.

Adidas's new shoes are expected to retail at $US500 ($694) when they are made available to the general public later this year.

Some athletes have complained that super shoes are tantamount to "technological doping", while and Australian marathon great Robert de Castella called for the "ludicrous" shoes to be banned as they go against the "spirit" of athletics.

Both Yomif Kejelcha (left) and Sabastian Sawe broke the two-hour mark in Adidas's new shoe. (Getty Images: Warren Little)

However, World Athletics president Lord Coe said his organisation had a duty to "enable" innovation.

"I don't think any society, any civilisation, any sector of the economy has been served well if you try to strangle innovation," Coe told the BBC.

Loading...

"The role of World Athletics is very clear: we want to enable, but we also have a regulatory responsibility.

"Yes, shoes play a part, but not the biggest part.

"The biggest part is the mentality of the athlete, the physicality of the athlete, the world-class coaching, the world-class programmes that are now being run through federations to support their athletes. That's all a part of the improved performances."

Critics say the rapid advancement in shoe technology and associated decrease in times meant that it was impossible to assuage whether improvements were legally achieved or assisted by doping.

The world-record-breaking super shoe was lauded the day after the London Marathon. (Getty Images: In Pictures/Richard Baker)

Professor Ross Tucker, host of The Real Science of Sport podcast, wrote on X that the new super shoes "confound the analyses and make the doping issue harder to assess".

Sawe, it should be noted, has taken proactive steps to remove any doping suspicion by funding enhanced testing through the Athletics Integrity Unit.

That has been funded to the tune of $US50,000 by Adidas, who are desperate to ensure that any improvements in Sawe's time — and, as a result, the part their shoes played in it — are not tainted by the stench of performance enhancing drugs.

Why we can believe in the unbelievable Sabastian Sawe smashes one of athletics' seemingly unbreakable barriers by running a marathon faster than two hours.

World Athletics has installed limits on technology in the past.

The body placed limits on sole thickness (no more than 40 millimetres thick), the design of carbon-fibre plates, and mandated that any shoes are available to be purchased by the general public.

"This is inevitably an evolutionary process," Coe said.

"It's only been relatively recently that we've had a system of evaluation.

"We work closely with the athletes, the coaches, the shoe companies. We don't want them to go off and spend hundreds of millions of dollars on shoes that we're going to find illegal. So there is a balance."

Sebastian Coe said World Athletics should assist in the development of shoe technology. (Getty Images: PA Images/Martin Rickett)

Sawe, for his part, said his improvement has been down to his ability to run more than 200km a week at altitude and his fuelling strategy during the race.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Coe said that intensive training regime could also be put down to improvements in technology.

"We often overlook that with the design to improve performance goes a lot of biomechanical work around injury prevention," the 69-year-old said.

"The athletes are able to train for longer, they're able to race longer, they're able to be in our sport for longer, and that has to be a good thing."

Coe acknowledged that there were understandable concerns over the advancement of technology within the sport and striking the right balance between development and remaining true to the sport's roots.

"Life is always about balances," Coe said.

"I think at World Athletics we have technical teams that are always going to be conscious of where that balance is. At the moment, I think we're the right side of it."

title: Octogenarian scuba diver not ready to hang up fins yet
text:
Jenny Dachroeden had always wanted to scuba dive but was too busy raising three children and running a business. That is, until she turned 47.

"By that time, the boys had grown, I'd gotten rid of the businesses, and I had the time," Ms Dachroeden said.

She signed up for a scuba course on the Gold Coast and "got hooked". That was in 1990.

"It was amazing. When you get down there and realise how wonderful it is and the things that you see, it blows everybody away," she said.

More than three decades and about 3,000 dives later, she's explored oceans around the world, including the Maldives, Palau, Papua New Guinea, Mexico and the United States.

Her underwater adventures have taken her to towering kelp forests, shipwrecks teeming with marine life, down oil rigs to pry off scallops to eat raw with soy sauce, and into quarries to feed packets of sausages to giant albino catfish.

At 83, Ms Dachroeden continues to enjoy her hobby off Queensland's Gold Coast and the far north, but says two hip replacements in the past year have slowed her down.

"I prefer now at this age to dive in warm water, see pretty coral, take a few pictures," she said.

" I'm not into doing the 'hot dog' stuff that I did when I was younger. "

Jenny Dachroeden continued diving during a six-year stint living two hours from the ocean in the Californian desert. (Supplied)

How old is too old to dive?

Aside from restrictions imposed by individual dive operators and insurance companies, there is no industry-wide upper age limit for scuba diving.

The Guinness World Records for the oldest scuba divers were set by a 100-year-old man in 2020 and a 94-year-old woman in 2022.

But does the sport get riskier with age?

It's not a simple question to answer, according to Joel Hissink, who ran the Royal Australian Navy's submarine and underwater medicine unit from 2012 to 2015.

Dr Hissink said older divers were often more experienced and risk-averse, and many dived safely into their 80s and even 90s.

But a recent review of Australia's 430 scuba diving fatalities in the past 50 years found while the number of annual deaths remained stable at about eight a year, the age of the casualties had increased, and the proportion of cardiac-related deaths had more than doubled.

Byron Bay GP Joel Hissink used to run the Royal Australian Navy submarine and underwater medicine unit. (Supplied: Joel Hissink)

Dr Hissink said aging increased the risk of cardiovascular issues such as heart attacks and strokes across the general population as cholesterol built up in the arteries.

But he said scuba diving could put additional strain on someone's heart, for example, if they were having to swim against a strong current or deal with an emergency situation.

"When you're in cool water, your peripheral blood flow gets restricted and channelled back to the central part of your body, and that can increase the load on your heart as well," he said.

"So if your heart is already not at 100 per cent, scuba diving can worsen the function just by being submerged in the water."

He said while some conditions were "not compatible" with scuba diving, such as epilepsy and lung surgery, the activity should be largely accessible, regardless of age.

" I am an advocate for not restricting scuba diving to anyone … it really is case-by-case. "

But Dr Hissink said it was imperative to be properly assessed by a diving doctor, particularly for those who had attained their dive ticket many years ago.

Jenny Dachroeden on the Great Barrier Reef in 2016. (Supplied)

The South Pacific Underwater Medicine Society, which represents hundreds of diving and hyperbaric medical practitioners across Australasia, recommends divers over 45 have a dive medical at least every five years.

While not legally required, Dr Hissink said it made sense for dive operators to subject older guests to more stringent checks, given their duty of care to both the individual and their own staff.

"You don't want people diving if there's a high likelihood of them requiring rescue, for instance, because that's not fair on their staff," he said.

Cairns-based Spirit of Freedom is one of the Queensland companies that requires guests over the age of 65 to have completed a dive medical in the past 12 months.

The company's Britney Stevens said, anecdotally, the age profile of guests had gradually trended older over the past decade.

Jenny Dachroeden travelled to the Coral Sea in late 2025 with dive operator Spirit of Freedom. (ABC News: Sharnie Kim)

"Diving is a pretty expensive hobby, and I think with a little bit more age comes a little bit more money usually, so they have the money to come out to the reef," she said.

She said the crew checked conditions before each dive and advised some guests against participating if there were strong currents or other factors increasing the difficulty of the dive.

"We have needed to ask people to sit out, but typically they understand," she said.

Not ready to hang up fins yet

While keen to continue her favourite pastime, Ms Dachroeden said it may become more difficult, particularly to overseas destinations, because some travel insurers do not offer scuba diving cover past a certain age.

"I'm fortunate that I'm still insured, but that may not last much longer," she said.

"When they're not taking into account the condition or the fitness of the person, and they're taking it purely by age, that's not fair."

Jenny Dachroeden and her diving friends, aged in their 60s and 70s, call themselves the "Golden Oldies". (Supplied: Jenny Dachroeden)

But with thousands of dives under her belt, she hoped to inspire seniors not to quit due to age alone.

She said the key was maintaining one's health and fitness and not being "stupid".

"I'm diving shallower now and … if I felt there was going to be a little too much current, I sat out," she said.

title: Piastri qualifies third for Miami sprint, Norris pole
text:
Formula 1 world champion Lando Norris has taken pole position for the Miami Grand Prix sprint race, with Mercedes's Kimi Antonelli second fastest to deny McLaren a one-two on the starting grid.

Norris lapped the Hard Rock Stadium with a best time of one minute 27.869 seconds, beating last year's sprint pole sitter and current title leader by 0.222.

"It was great. Perfect result and a nice way to reward the team," Norris said on track.

"We've got a lot of new upgrades on the car. It's nice to feel some grip again.

"It's nice to reward the guys and the girls who have put a lot of work into it.

"It's a good result for us, but it's still the beginning of the weekend. [There is] still a long way to go, but it's nice to kick it off by doing this."

Norris is the first driver to deny Mercedes the top spot in a qualifying race this season.

McLaren's Oscar Piastri will line up third, just 0.017 seconds behind Antonelli.

Oscar Piastri (left) was just pipped for a spot on the front row. (Getty Images: Formula 1/Mark Sutton)

Ferrari's Charles Leclerc will start alongside the Australian on the second row for the 19-lap race on Saturday, local time.

Max Verstappen pipped George Russell to fifth, with Lewis Hamilton's Ferarri set to start sixth.

"Pretty surprising how big a jump McLaren and Ferrari made. That's pretty damn impressive," Russell, who won the season opener in Australia, said.

"From my side, I've been struggling. Miami is not a track I love, especially in those hotter conditions but it's only Sprint Qualifying, so let's see what tomorrow brings."

Franco Colapinto, Isack Hadjar and Pierre Gasly round out the top 10.

There has been no racing in F1 since the Japanese Grand Prix in March, with the Bahrain and Saudi Arabia races called off due to the Iran war.

Piastri had just found his feet in Japan after a nightmare start to the year, having failed to make the starting grid in Melbourne and Shanghai.

In Suzuka, the 25-year-old burst from the blocks and finished runner-up to Mercedes ace Kimi Antonelli.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Norris said although he expected his McLaren to work nicely on the track, the upgrades for this weekend had clearly worked.

McLaren brought a major upgrade package to this race, as did most other teams on the grid, although Mercedes did not.

"We've known that this track has always been good to us but we knew that what we were bringing was hopefully going to be a good step, so it's nice that our estimations have proved correct," Norris said.

"Since the first lap today, I've felt comfortable and I thought, 'Oh, I've got a bit of rear grip.'

"[It's] good to start the weekend with a pole."

The main grand prix, the fourth round of the season, is on Sunday (Monday morning AEST).

ABC/Reuters

title: Dingo sightings near primary school put parents on edge
text:
Sightings of dingoes at a Pilbara primary school have left "stressed-out" parents questioning whether children should be walking around the town by themselves.

Dingoes were reported at Dampier Primary School days before the beginning of term two and the City of Karratha issued a social media notice about the animals.

Dampier father Phil Shiner said he had seen dingoes during the day at the end of his street.

"They were more desensitised to people," he said.

"You start to second-guess whether you should send [your children] out by themselves to go to their friend's house."

Phil Shiner is concerned about his children walking around outside. (Supplied: Phil Shiner)

Laura Dent, whose four-year-old son attends the primary school, said it was "pretty scary" and that a lot of her friends were "stressed out" by the sightings.

"Kids in Dampier just live out in the streets, riding and going to each others' houses," she said.

" You think, 'OK, something bad could have happened', especially with how close it was to the school. "

Ms Dent said the school had responded by keeping the children off the oval and having the children eat inside.

It also asked parents to pick up their kids to prevent them from lingering in the area.

The Department of Education did not respond to questions from the ABC about dingo sightings at the school, instead referring them to the City of Karratha and the Department of Primary Industries and Regional Development.

'Don't befriend dingoes'

City of Karratha regulatory services manager Simon Hubbard said the council did not know where the dingoes had come from.

"We have been liaising with the education department because we are aware that they have been seen in and around the primary school," he said.

"There's conjecture around why they may be coming in, but essentially we think they've got a readily available food source in Dampier, which is what's making them hang around there.

" We ask people to refrain from feeding them. "

Why the dingoes have turned up is unclear. (ABC News: Chris Lewis — file photo)

Murdoch University Harry Butler Institute ecologist Trish Fleming said there could be several reasons for the presence of the dingoes.

"It could be that there's a single group of animals that have come through that are moving through the area," she said.

"Or, it could be that there's been a few females locally who've been really successful last year in breeding, which might mean that the numbers of dingoes have increased.

"The young animals will leave the den and become independent eight to nine months … so this is a good time for them … to be finding a new place to live."

Dampier dingo destroyed

Last week a dingo euthanased at Karijini National Park following attacks on small children caused uproar on social media.

The City of Karratha has since confirmed a locally captured dingo had been euthanased.

Social media had mixed responses to a dingo captured in Karijini being euthanised. (ABC News)

"They definitely need to be out of town, but it does make me a bit sad they had to be euthanised," Ms Dent said.

Local wildlife enthusiast Heather Osborne was also saddened the dingo had been destroyed despite no attacks or aggression being reported.

"I believe we've got a responsibility to learn how to co-exist safely with the wildlife around us. It's their home too," she said.

" We need to explore humane and effective solutions first. Is killing our only possible solution? I don't know if it is. "

Rose Best says dingoes in the Pilbara are less of a worry than some other wildlife. (ABC Pilbara: Kimberley Putland)

Pilbara Wildlife Carers Association chair Rose Best said dingoes were widely misunderstood.

"Our Pilbara dingoes are quite different [from K'gari] — they are very timid, very shy," she said.

"Yes, they'll walk the streets, but if you make a noise, they will run."

Rose Best, seen here with Shilo, says dingoes get a bad rap. (ABC Pilbara: Kimberley Putland)

Ms Best, who has a pet dingo, said it was important that people were vigilant, but dingoes were less of a threat than other wildlife.

title: As I sat for an Archibald entry, I tried to assume an air of dignity
text:
Perhaps I should have heeded the warning of John Singer Sargent.

He was the greatest portrait painter of the late 1800s Gilded Age, and one of the most revered of all times, but Sargent was only in his 30s when he realised the great danger in the unbalanced equation of vanity plus truth that was his life's work.

"Every time I paint a portrait, I lose a friend," he sighed.

As I stood in the studio of one of my oldest and dearest friends, awkwardly holding the pose for an entry into the Archibald Portrait prize for 2026, I prayed that Sargent was wrong.

Stieg Persson and Virginia Trioli have been friends for years. Will a portrait session be too much to handle?

I also remembered that Sargent said something about a portrait not being about getting a "likeness". Now I started to wonder what on earth I'd got myself into.

Turns out I need not have worried: that portrait of me by the contemporary artist, Stieg Persson, has been shortlisted for the Archibald.

The Archibald Prize is the Mardi Gras of Australian visual art.

Virginia and Cora by Stieg Persson (AGNSW: Stieg Persson)

Held at the Art Gallery of New South Wales, it is really the only time that the nation pays combined attention to painting, and it's remarkable that it should be for a competition in the arcane tradition of portrait painting.

Since 1921, when the editor of the Bulletin, JF Archibald, founded the prize, the trustees of the AGNSW have awarded the best portrait of a "distinguished figure from art, letters, science or politics".

The competition has specific rules: a time frame the work must be started and completed in; and a requirement that the work be painted "from life" — you must stand, feeling like a fool, in front of that artist for as long as they need you.

With iPhone around what's the point of a painted portrait?

I'd been painted for the Archibald once before, back in the early oughts, by another old friend, the artist John Campbell, and that one had been politely declined but it ended up in the slightly groovier "rejection" exhibition, the Salon des Refuses.

This one, however, had been a long time in the making.

My friend, the artist Stieg Persson, and I have spent many years tossing about the idea of an Archibald entry.

Sign up for Weekend Reads in your inbox Start your weekend with the best of the ABC's journalism, presented by Virginia Trioli. Discover compelling features, big ideas and revealing analysis to understand the stories that matter to Australians.

We are both fascinated by the traditions and language of portraiture: subject, composition, light and meaning. But we are also intrigued by the uneasy relationship of favour and flattery that defines the painter/portrait relationship. From the Medicis to the Meyers, the uncomfortable system of working for rich patrons nonetheless produced great and immortal paintings.

Along with the other 19th century greats, Sargent is one of our favourites. A painter of immense delicacy and careful touch, he was also capable of a keen psychological realism, even though he said he only ever painted what was in front of him.

The rich and powerful of London, Paris and New York commissioned Sargent for a work of beauty and flattery — and that's what they got, but they also inadvertently received a visual essay in how strongly they strove for taste, class and standing.

But what is the point of a painted portrait now, when an iPhone can cut out the sly intervention of the artist and provide more of that flattery, with filtering, than even the shrewdest court artist could?

As Rachel Griffiths discovered in her excellent series on the Archibald Prize, there is something about the transaction between artist and sitter — a vulnerability, a trust and a declaration — that continues to draw crowds of Australians, year on year.

Sometimes it's to exclaim at the skill of the photo-realist painters — how do they make paint look like a Polaroid? Or to see if some undisclosed truth is revealed in the portrait of their favourite celebrity.

The painter's skill is still, after thousands of years of the art, a thing of wonder, and I love seeing a visitor lean slowly towards the canvas, eyes narrowing then widening — how did they paint that?

I tried to assume an air of dignity

On my first visit to the studio, I was taken aback by the stack of new canvases Stieg had obtained. These smaller formats were to be all the studies for the final, large canvas: attempts at bits of head and back, several versions of Cora, tests with techniques of colour and glazing.

This was going to be a lot of work.

On my first visit to the studio, I was taken aback by the stack of smaller formats to be studies for the final painting. (Supplied)

My job was simply to stand and project… what? I tried to assume some sort of air, a feeling of presence, dignity. But after a while you completely forget what your face is doing and just fall into reverie. Maybe that's the artist's skill: waiting and watching for that moment.

It is an undoubtedly romantic feeling, standing still and listening to the almost silent brushstrokes, only because of the incredible history that stretches behind you: every great portrait you can think of started this way.

I love that Stieg captured my dog Cora's customary look of bewilderment, and that the picture celebrates many aspects of Melbourne in the one frame, including a design by the Melbourne-born couturier, Martin Grant.

The work now sits in the exhibition alongside people I know and want to know and will tour regionally after several months in Sydney. Go and see the show, pick a favourite and widen your eyes before a format that a six-inch screen could never hope to match.

This weekend, let's remain in art mode with a couple of terrific reads on some well-known street art and a possible art fraud.

Have a safe and happy weekend; this is what I'll be playing as I dance around the room with my reluctant dog Cora, in just a little celebration of an age-old tradition. Go well.

Virginia Trioli is presenter of Creative Types and a former co-host of ABC News Breakfast and Mornings on ABC Radio Melbourne.

title: ‘Just like North Sydney’: The public pool facing a $43 million cost blowout
text:
Advertisement

NationalNSWSydney councils ‘Just like North Sydney’: The public pool facing a $43 million cost blowout Ellie Busby May 2, 2026 — 1:30pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

The price tag to redevelop a public pool in the city’s north-west has soared from $30 million to more than $70 million in four years, drawing comparisons with cost blowouts on the North Sydney Olympic Pool project. Hawkesbury City Council will have to find $43.1 million through “alternate financing arrangements” to plug the funding gap for its redevelopment of Richmond Swimming Centre, after the council approved the addition of a gymnasium and the expansion of the outdoor swimming pool from six to eight lanes. An artist’s impression of what the indoor pool would look like at the redeveloped Richmond Swimming Centre. Hawkesbury City Council Announced in 2022, the pool upgrade was originally costed at $30 million, to be fully funded through the state government’s western Sydney infrastructure grants program, known then as WestInvest. But rising construction costs and changes in the project’s scope have pushed that figure to $73 million, a Hawkesbury City Council spokesperson said. The price tag has been criticised by many in the community, including some on the council.

Advertisement

Independent councillor Nathan Zamprogno said it was disappointing that the community was led to believe the project could be fully funded through the WestInvest grant, expressing that the cost will continue to grow. “What do we do when the costs reach $80 million, $90 million or more?” he said. “This is going to become a millstone around the community’s neck, just like the North Sydney pool debacle, and is a bitter pill at a time when the council is also seeking to raise everybody’s rates by another 40 per cent.” After two years of redevelopment became five, the new North Sydney Olympic Pool will have cost $122 million when it opens later this year.

Advertisement

Hawkesbury City Council has applied for a special rate variation of 8.66 per cent a year for four years to fund various infrastructure renewal and upgrade projects. Related Article Exclusive

Sydney councils Inside North Sydney pool as it inches closer to ‘the next big milestone’ The decision to proceed with the redevelopment of Richmond pool occurred at an April council meeting that stretched to 12.41am and included general manager Elizabeth Richardson tendering her resignation. The Herald is not suggesting these matters were related. Built in the 1960s, the ageing pool is approaching the end of its life and lacks “many of the modern expectations” around accessibility, safety and energy efficiency, the council spokesperson said. “A like-for-like replacement would not meet the requirements of the western Sydney infrastructure funding agreement, resulting in the need to return the funding,” they said.

Advertisement

“Council would be then required to either fully fund the replacement or consider permanent closure of the centre.” The pool was one of 10 projects in the Hawkesbury that received funding through the WestInvest program. The ageing pool is approaching the “end of its useful life”, a council spokesperson said. Sitthixay Ditthavong In a January 2023 media release, former mayor Sarah McMahon said residents could “expect work to begin over the next 12 months” on the projects, including Richmond Swimming Centre. However, that didn’t happen, and the pool is now not expected to be complete until late 2028, “weather permitting”, the spokesperson said.



Advertisement

Along with a gymnasium and expanded outdoor pool, the redeveloped swimming centre is to feature an indoor 25-metre pool, kids “splash park”, new bathrooms and a cafe. The Sydney Morning Herald has a bureau in the heart of Parramatta. Email parramatta@smh.com.au with news tips.

title: RA is set for a World Cup windfall. It must splash the cash on junior development
text:
Advertisement

Analysis SportRugby UnionRugby World Cup RA is set for a World Cup windfall. It must splash the cash on junior development Paul Cully May 2, 2026 — 1:30pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Rugby Australia’s revenue of $262 million for the 2025 British and Irish Lions year is a significant number for several reasons. It allowed RA to announce a massive surplus of $70.6 million and wipe out its large debt, but it is also a useful comparison tool with their Bledisloe Cup rivals across the ditch. Finn Mackay is a significant talent, but will Rugby Australia be able to help him reach his full potential? Queensland Reds Coincidentally, that $262 million is in the same neighbourhood as New Zealand Rugby’s annual income. But when the Kiwis announce their annual results next week, they will likely only break even (or possibly show a small surplus) after pouring it all back into the game. So, there is a $70 million funding gap between the two countries, and New Zealand again capitalised on that investment advantage this week.

Advertisement

The Australian Super Rugby teams were 0-3 during Super Round in Christchurch, and the Junior Wallabies lost 34-29 to the New Zealand under-20s in their opening match of the Rugby Championship under-20s tournament in South Africa. These two events are connected: an example of how systems beat natural talent or, more accurately, how natural talent is not Australian rugby’s problem – but it is also not enough on its own to produce winning Wallabies and Wallaroos teams. The natural talent referred to here is Junior Wallabies No.10 Finn Mackay, also of the Queensland Reds. The tall Mackay invites easy comparisons with Stephen Larkham, and those comparisons are justified. He has an eye for a gap, an almost languid running style and excellent pass selection at the line. So, celebration time for Wallabies fans? Not quite. When Mackay leaves the under-20s program his next few years will be in a spotty development system that could fail him, as it has others, unless serious dollars are invested in it.

Advertisement

Finn Mackay in action during the under-20 Rugby Championship match against New Zealand. Getty Images In fact, Mackay may already be a half-step behind his New Zealand counterpart, Mika Muliaina. Muliaina, the nephew of All Blacks centurion Mils Muliaina, probably doesn’t have Mackay’s God-given abilities, but his game management was exemplary when the young Australians were shown a 20-minute red card. In fact, when Mackay threw a risky pass that was intercepted on the hour mark with his side leading 29-17, Muliaina identified space and executed a perfect cross-field kick deep in his own half during the play that turned the game on its head. The Kiwis went upfield, Junior Wallabies halfback Angus Grover received a yellow card for a supposedly cynical ruck infringement, and the New Zealanders scored 17 unanswered points. Muliaina showed the benefits of already playing at a higher level among men. Last year, he featured eight times for Southland in New Zealand’s National Provincial Championship, starting most of those games in the No.10 jersey.

Advertisement

He’ll likely do the same this year, and by the time he makes his Super Rugby debut for the Highlanders, Muliaina could have more than 20 NPC games under his belt. Mackay won’t get that schooling or the opportunity to make mistakes in a competition less intense than Super Rugby but still far harder than the under-20s. The development of Mika Muliaina, pictured in action for Southland in the NPC in 2025, is in stark contrast to Finn Mackay. Getty Images Instead, he’ll likely be fast-tracked into Super Rugby with high expectations. That’s not the Reds’ fault – and they have been good at creating more game time for their younger players – but they are working within the constraints of the current structure. This all came to mind when listening to a recent Rugby Unity podcast featuring Eddie Jones and Ewen McKenzie (incidentally, the passion and knowledge shown by both men strongly suggest neither is finished with Australian rugby and has plenty to offer, if bygones can be bygones).

Advertisement

They observed that Australian rugby needed heavy investment in development. That has two parts: first, talented young players must be secured; second, they need a meaningful competition to play more games in. Related Article Force While code wars rage in the east, Force and Bears are working together in the west Waratahs coach Dan McKellar recently pleaded for more rugby, a theme that resonates among the players. The Herald understands Brumbies flanker Rory Scott will be the next Australian to spend the Super Rugby off-season in the NPC, signing for Tasman. NZ Rugby can afford to invest much more heavily in the game as a whole because its yearly revenue, excluding World Cup years, equates to what RA enjoyed in a Lions year. However, another windfall is coming to Australia next year with the World Cup. At that point, the funds will be so significant that RA will have to make some investment decisions; simply saving them for a proverbial rainy day won’t suffice. The past week has emphatically shown where the money must go, so that players such as Finn Mackay are given as much chance to succeed as the New Zealand players they clearly match in talent.

title: From unwanted to unstoppable: How Donaldson is playing his way back into the Wallabies
text:
Advertisement

SportRugby UnionWallabies From unwanted to unstoppable: How Donaldson is playing his way back into the Wallabies Jonathan Drennan May 2, 2026 — 1:30pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Ben Donaldson was on his sofa in Australia last November when the Wallabies faced France in Paris in the final Test of the end-of-season European tour. The Force five-eighth wasn’t injured. He just wasn’t wanted. His stock had fallen so low that inside centre Hamish Stewart, who had played just 10 professional games at five-eighth, was selected to fill in as the reserve No.10 at the Stade de France. Ben Donaldson was at the centre of the Force’s win against the Waratahs. Getty Images Six months on, after helping the Force to defeat the Waratahs on Friday night, Donaldson’s performances are on the rise again. His last appearance for the Wallabies came in the victory over the British and Irish Lions in the third Test last August, when he came off the bench to kick two crucial conversions. But Donaldson is honest about the reasons behind Joe Schmidt’s decision to leave him out of the Wallabies’ touring squad.

Advertisement

The coach is an admirer of Donaldson’s attacking skill set, but was concerned about his defence. That assessment remains valid. Against the Waratahs on Friday night, Donaldson made three from three tackles, but has a relatively weak tackle success rate of 64 per cent this season. In contrast, Queensland Reds five-eighth Carter Gordon’s tackle success rate is 85 per cent. It should also be noted that Donaldson has played 10 games of Super Rugby to Gordon’s five after an injury-interrupted year. Carter Gordon remains a better defensive option than Donaldson, but has not taken goal kicks this season. Getty Images While Donaldson has his defensive frailties, he is compensating heavily in other areas. After kicking a straightforward 68th-minute penalty that ultimately sealed Friday night’s victory for the Force, Donaldson has a 85 per cent success rate from the kicking tee this season. By comparison, Harry McLaughlin-Phillips and Tane Edmed, respectively of the Reds and Brumbies, are sitting on 80 per cent and 75 per cent success rates. Gordon is nursing a minor knee injury and hasn’t taken a shot at goal this year.

Advertisement

Donaldson’s threat as a running playmaker was also on display against the Waratahs, when he carried the ball 50 metres, made two line breaks and beat three defenders. Donaldson later admitted it had been a conscious decision to think outside the team’s game plan and take on the Waratahs’ defence. “I’m just driving the team, trying to play free, play what’s in front of me and not be too robotic,” Donaldson said. “That was my mindset coming into tonight: touch the ball as much as possible, run at the line, and sometimes I get to go through and make a line break. They are a few of my ‘work-ons’. Related Article Analysis

Rugby World Cup RA is set for a World Cup windfall. It must splash the cash on junior development “In terms of Joe and the Wallabies, obviously, he wants me to be a director and a loud voice, but he actually wants the 10 touching the ball as well. He wants 10s stepping up and not sitting in the pocket.” Donaldson is the outstanding Australian playmaker for kicking distance. Before facing the Waratahs, the Force five-eighth was averaging 331 metres kicked per game. In Test rugby, his ability to help the team exit effectively and also kick for the corner is an asset. His next nearest Australian rivals are Brumbies Declan Meredith and Edmed, who average 230 and 211 metres respectively.

Advertisement

With the Wallabies due to face Ireland in Sydney on July 4, the window for playmakers to press their claim is narrowing. Gordon is expected to return from injury imminently, while his Reds teammate Tom Lynagh has managed one Super appearance off the bench this season. There are also overseas options in Noah Lolesio, who is plying his trade in Japan’s second division, and veteran James O’Connor at Leicester. Donaldson said after defeating the Waratahs that the Wallabies have not been in his thoughts this season. After leading the Force to their first victory in Sydney in five years, it’s safe to say he will be back in the thoughts of Schmidt. title: Sea Eagles teenager gets his chance against Nathan Cleary
text:

SportNRLNRL 2026 Sea Eagles teenager gets his chance against Nathan Cleary Scott Bailey May 2, 2026 — 12:48pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Kieran Foran has made the biggest call of his rookie coaching career, declaring teenager Joey Walsh is ready to start as an NRL half against four-time Penrith premiership winner and NSW Origin champion Nathan Cleary. Regular Manly half Jamal Fogarty was ruled out of the Sea Eagles bumper round nine clash against Penrith at CommBank Stadium on Sunday evening with a groin injury. Joey Walsh will make his NRL run-on debut against Nathan Cleary on Sunday. Peter Rae It means, Walsh will be handed one of the toughest starting debuts in the game, set to go head-to-head with Cleary. “He’s got endless natural ability,” Manly’s interim coach Foran said of 19-year-old Walsh.

“On top of that he is a wonderful student of the game, he works extremely hard. He is a tough kid and is really courageous and competitive. “He just ticks so many boxes. Without putting too much pressure on him, we have high hopes for Joey’s future in this game. We love what he is about. Manly interim coach Kieran Foran says Walsh is ready for regular NRL football. Wolter Peeters “And tomorrow night he gets to go out there and do it. And we have all the belief in the world he is going to get out there and get the job done.” The Sea Eagles had given Fogarty until yesterday’s captain’s run to prove his fitness and are hopeful he will face Brisbane next week after limping off late against Parramatta.

Walsh is viewed as Manly’s future, with the former Australian schoolboys rugby union representative playing 19 minutes off the bench in his NRL debut in Daly Cherry-Evans’ farewell game at the end of last season. Loading Teenage playmaker Onitoni Large is also viewed as a long-term prospect. Fogarty has been one of Manly’s best this year, while Brooks has improved significantly through the Sea Eagles’ four-straight wins since Foran took over. “He has been knocking on the door for some time and has known he’s thereabouts,” Foran said of Walsh. Sitthixay Ditthavong “It was really important he kept working on his game in NSW Cup and building parts of his game we wanted him to work on. “He’s been playing really well. I said to him a few weeks back your opportunity will come at some point through injury or what not, and it’s come this week.”

Related Article NRL 2026 ‘I left Bulldogs in better place’: Mahoney puts Canterbury exit behind him The other aspect of Manly’s future is their next coach, with Foran yet to start talks on taking over full-time after his faultless start. The Sea Eagles have the Panthers, Brisbane and Wests Tigers in the next three weeks, in what looms as their biggest tests so far under the Manly great. “I’ve got a bit of a beef with ‘Foz’ there, he’s making our job look very easy, when we’re always trying to tell everyone how hard it is,” Penrith coach Ivan Cleary quipped this week. “It’s an amazing achievement, isn’t it?

“It’s obviously early in his coaching journey but clearly he knows what he’s doing and has got the full respect of the team.” AAP

title: State’s first comprehensive cancer centre on the way for WA
text:
Advertisement

NationalWAHealthcare State’s first comprehensive cancer centre on the way for WA Rebecca Peppiatt May 2, 2026 — 12:34pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Western Australia will soon be able to provide state-of-the-art care to cancer patients after the announcement that a comprehensive centre will soon be built at the Queen Elizabeth II Medical Centre in Nedlands. The news was delivered on Saturday, revealing that world-leading research will now be available in the state in the same way that it is at the Chris O’Brien Lifehouse in Sydney and the Peter MacCallum Cancer Centre in Melbourne which integrate cancer treatment, world-class cancer research and clinical trial capacity in one place. WA will get its own comprehensive cancer centre, similar to those in other States and internationally, with the WA State Government confirming funding for the Perkins WA Comprehensive Cancer Centre to be built at the QEII Medical Campus site. Once constructed the Perkins WA Comprehensive Cancer Centre will include inpatient and outpatient services, research and education services including expanded clinical trial beds and a Cancer Care Coordination Centre (CCCC) providing care coordination, patient navigation and patient information services. Harry Perkins Institute of Medical Research CEO, Professor Peter Leedman, said the Perkins WA Comprehensive Cancer Centre would transform the way cancer research and care is delivered throughout the state.

Advertisement

“Around the world, comprehensive cancer centres that truly integrate cutting-edge research with patient care are life-changing for so many people,” he said. “The difference between a truly comprehensive cancer centre, like the Peter MacCallum Cancer Centre in Victoria, and existing cancer services in WA, stems from deep integration of world-leading medical research and clinical trials into the treatment and care provided to patients. “Research shows that strong collaboration between scientists and clinicians leads to significantly better patient outcomes and a more coordinated, less fragmented cancer journey.” In WA, one in three people will die from cancer and more than 14,000 cases will be diagnosed each year. Premier Roger Cook made the official announcement that the state government would be backing the centre on Saturday ahead of next week’s budget. He said the facility will transform care and support for Western Australians facing their toughest challenge.

Advertisement

“Most Western Australians have experienced the hard road that is cancer treatment – either themselves or a loved one,” he said. “Our backing of the Perkins WA Comprehensive Cancer Centre is about ensuring that when a family faces their toughest fight, they have the very best medical technology, research, and support services right here at home. “This project cements Western Australia’s reputation as a global leader in medical research and innovation, attracting the world’s brightest clinical minds to our state while creating hundreds of local jobs during construction and beyond.” Related Article Vaccination Poliovirus strain detected in WA for the first time The investment comes at the same time as the Cook Labor Government is boosting the Building Hospitals Fund by $500 million to $2 billion in the 2026-27 Budget, as part of the Government’s $5.5 billion hospital constriction program over the next four years. Planning for the Perkins WA Comprehensive Cancer Centre, including the final scope and design, will start following a masterplan for the QEII Medical Precinct, to guide potential future projects at the Nedlands Health Hub.

Advertisement

Perkins Chair, Ben Morton, said helping people with cancer live better and longer was at the core of the Perkins WA Comprehensive Cancer Centre. “This centre will help deliver better research outcomes targeted at finding cures and better treatments – while at the same time improving the care of people fighting cancer,” he said. “We take our responsibility for developing the Perkins WA Comprehensive Cancer Centre very seriously and are honoured to be entrusted by both the WA State and Federal Governments to deliver this transformational project.” The centre will include 90 multi-day beds, plus expansion for a further 30 beds, 40 same-day beds and chairs including chemotherapy and haematology and up to eight intensive care/high dependency beds. Outpatient services will include 36 ambulatory care / specialist clinics, space for two radiation oncology bunkers, four operating theatres, as well as space for two further theatres, two procedure rooms, and seven imaging services including CT, MRI and PET. The research and education services will include expanded clinical trial beds in addition to the existing six beds and 18 chairs in Linear Clinical Research at QEII Medical Centre, a variety of wet laboratory and dry research spaces, function-specific embedded research units and a cancer tissue biobank. The Cancer Care Coordination Centre (CCCC) will provide care coordination, patient navigation and patient information services including virtual health services providing support for regional, remote and Aboriginal patients living with cancer, and working with WA Country Health Service (WACHS) Command Centre.
